title: The Eternal Decree of God, Part 1
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Eternal Decree of God_ Part 1 (Pastor Tito Lyro).txt
outline: God is absolutely sovereign — of the essence of being God
God's sovereignty is not reactive but by eternal appointment
WCF Chapter 3: the eternal decree — God ordained whatsoever comes to pass
Four-Sunday series: sovereignty of God in the context of the eternal decree
First lesson: the nature and scope of the decree
summary: Opening lesson of a four-part January Sunday School series on the eternal decree of God. Establishes that God's sovereignty is not reactive (like a parent chasing a toddler) but consists in His having appointed everything before creation. Uses Westminster Confession of Faith Chapter 3, paragraph 1 as the guide: God from all eternity freely and unchangeably ordained whatever comes to pass, yet without being the author of sin or violating the will of creatures.
transcript:
God is absolutely sovereign. I don't know if you realize that, but that is what the Bible teaches. And that is the case because being absolutely sovereign is of the essence of being God. One cannot be God and not be sovereign over all things — at least not the God of the Bible. God controls what happens in every moment of every day, everywhere.

But what we often picture is a reactive control. Remember when those of you who have kids went through the toddler stage — when your kids start walking and you go along behind them, trying to control where they go and protect them? Sometimes we picture God doing that: reacting to things happening in the world, trying to make sure things don't go the wrong way, busy pushing this thing here, pulling that thing there. But that is not how God is sovereign. He is sovereign by appointing all things before he even created anything. That is how God's sovereignty is established. He decreed — that is, he set in place — everything that was going to happen, even before he created anything. Even when he was the only being in existence, in his Triune existence, he appointed all things.

So we are going to spend today and the next three Sundays — the month of January — talking about the sovereignty of God in the context of the decree of God. We are going to use our Confession to help guide us.

Now you might say, why use the Confession? Because no one worth listening to comes on a Sunday without any preparation, without having read anything, without having planned anything, and just opens the Bible. If you're a Quaker, I suppose you do that. But anyone worth listening to uses some structure. We use what we say we believe as a good structure. Also, the fourth thing you promise when you become a member is to live with the confessional standards of the church — and yet many who make that promise have never actually read them. So this is a good way to expose everyone to what it is we believe and confess.

## The Big Idea

Here is the big idea I want to convey in this lesson: God from all eternity, by his most wise and holy counsel, freely and unchangeably ordained whatever comes to pass — without being the cause and source of sin, and without destroying human responsibility.

There are at least three essential questions that flow from this:
1. What do the Scriptures and our Confession mean by "the decree of God"? Notice it is singular. We tend to say "the decrees of God," but our Confession speaks of the decree in the singular.
2. How can God ordain all things and yet remain perfectly holy?
3. Why must God's decree be eternal, immutable (that is, unchanging), and comprehensive — with nothing left outside of it?

By the time we finish this morning, I want you to be able to define God's eternal decree using Scripture and confessional language, explain how the decree relates to creation, providence, and redemption, and apply this doctrine to build one another up.

## The Westminster Standards on the Decree

We are anchoring this morning's teaching in the third chapter of the Confession, paragraph 1, which says:

> *"God from all eternity did, by the most wise and holy counsel of His own will, freely and unchangeably ordain whatsoever comes to pass; yet so as thereby neither is God the author of sin, nor is violence offered to the will of the creatures, nor is the liberty or contingency of second causes taken away, but rather established."*

The Shorter Catechism, in question 7, asks: "What are the decrees of God?" And answers: "The decrees of God are His eternal purpose, according to the counsel of His will, whereby, for His own glory, He hath foreordained whatsoever comes to pass."

The Larger Catechism addresses this in question 12, where it asks: "What are the decrees of God?" The answer: "God's decrees are the wise, free, and holy acts of the counsel of His will, whereby, from all eternity, He hath, for His own glory, unchangeably foreordained whatsoever comes to pass in time, especially concerning angels and men."

Because the Larger Catechism brings up angels and men specifically, it is worth pausing at two more questions. Question 18 asks: "What are God's works of providence?" Answer: "God's works of providence are His most holy, wise, and powerful preserving and governing all His creatures, ordering them, and all their actions, to His own glory." Providence is how God works out his decree in history.

Question 19 asks: "What is God's providence toward the angels?" Answer: "God by His providence permitted some of the angels, willfully and irrevocably, to fall into sin and damnation, limiting and ordering that, and all their sins, to His own glory, and established the rest in holiness and happiness." So God, from eternity past — before anything existed — ordained that Satan exists in the way he does.

## Why Does the Confession Begin with the Decree?

The logic of the Confession is this: Chapter 1 is on the Word of God. Chapter 2 is on God himself. Chapter 3 is on what God does. The Confession begins with God's decree in order to establish that God's will precedes all his works. God does not react to history. He ordained history. History unfolds according to his eternal purpose.

So: what is the decree of God? The decree of God is his eternal, wise, holy, and free plan by which he has ordained everything that comes to pass.

What does the Confession mean by saying that the decree is "free"? It means that nothing outside of God limited him when he made it. There are no special interests coming in saying, "God, you must do things this way." The only limiting factor is his own will. He is the only being in the universe of whom that is true.

Notice the key elements: the decree is from all eternity — it is not something that happened yesterday. It is wise and holy. It is freely ordained — nothing pushes God except himself. It is unchangeable. And it is comprehensive — it includes everything.

## Scripture Supports

Does the Bible teach this? Absolutely. Isaiah 46 records the Lord saying:

> *"Remember the former things of old, for I am God, and there is no other; I am God, and there is none like Me, declaring the end from the beginning, and from ancient times things that are not yet done."* (Isaiah 46:9–10)

"In the beginning" in the Old Testament refers to a time prior to creation — "In the beginning, God created." So he declares from before creation what is going to happen. And this is not saying, "I saw what was coming in the future." He is declaring what will happen because he has decreed it.

Isaiah 48:3 reinforces this:

> *"I have declared the former things from the beginning; they went forth from My mouth, and I caused them to hear it. Suddenly I did them, and they came to pass."* (Isaiah 48:3)

I declared them. Then I did them.

Jesus prays in John 17:24:

> *"Father, I desire that they also whom You gave Me may be with Me where I am, that they may behold My glory which You have given Me; for You loved Me before the foundation of the world."* (John 17:24)

The giving of God's people to Christ took place before the foundation of the world.

Turn to Ephesians 1, starting at verse 3, and we will read through verse 12:

> *"Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who has blessed us with every spiritual blessing in the heavenly places in Christ, just as He chose us in Him before the foundation of the world, that we should be holy and without blame before Him in love, having predestined us to adoption as sons by Jesus Christ to Himself, according to the good pleasure of His will, to the praise of the glory of His grace, by which He made us accepted in the Beloved. In Him we have redemption through His blood, the forgiveness of sins, according to the riches of His grace which He made to abound toward us in all wisdom and prudence, having made known to us the mystery of His will according to His good pleasure which He purposed in Himself, that in the dispensation of the fullness of the times He might gather together in one all things in Christ, both which are in heaven and which are on earth — in Him. In Him also we have obtained an inheritance, being predestined according to the purpose of Him who works all things according to the counsel of His will."* (Ephesians 1:3–11)

When you were brought to Christ, that was not something God decided to do on the spur of the moment. It was something he had decided from all eternity and then implemented in history. That is what providence is: the implementation in history of what God decreed before he created anything.

Notice too: "according to the good pleasure of His will." That is the "free" part. Nothing forced God to choose you. Nothing he saw in you made him say, "I need that person on my team." It is simply according to the good pleasure of his will.

Peter also teaches this in 1 Peter 1:17–21:

> *"...knowing that you were not redeemed with corruptible things, like silver or gold, from your aimless conduct received by tradition from your fathers, but with the precious blood of Christ, as of a lamb without blemish and without spot. He indeed was foreordained before the foundation of the world, but was manifest in these last times for you."* (1 Peter 1:18–20)

The work of Christ — everything connected to it — was ordained before creation.

There is a difference between declaring and decreeing. You can declare something you know about but have no part in bringing to pass. But to decree is to say: this is going to happen because I say so.

## Why Must the Decree Be Eternal and Immutable?

God's decree is eternal because God is eternal. God's decree is immutable because God is immutable. God does not change. If God changed from one position to another, it would mean the first position was not perfect — or that he is changing from perfect to not perfect. Either way it implies that God is not perfect. So his perfection requires that he also be immutable.

Romans 11:33–36 teaches that all things are from God and to God, and therefore eternal and immutable:

> *"Oh, the depth of the riches both of the wisdom and knowledge of God! How unsearchable are His judgments and His ways past finding out! For who has known the mind of the Lord? Or who has become His counselor? Or who has first given to Him and it shall be repaid to him? For of Him and through Him and to Him are all things, to whom be glory forever. Amen."* (Romans 11:33–36)

## God's Decree and the Problem of Sin

We all seem to be in agreement that God decreed whatever comes to pass — he literally wrote the script. But what do we do with sin? If God decreed everything, how is he not guilty of sin?

The answer that our Confession gives is this: God ordains all that comes to pass, yet is not the cause or culpable of sin. That is what the Confession means by saying God is not the "author" of sin — he is not morally culpable. Human responsibility and secondary causes remain intact.

James tells us that God is perfect in his holiness. James 1:13–17:

> *"Let no one say when he is tempted, 'I am tempted by God'; for God cannot be tempted by evil, nor does He Himself tempt anyone. But each one is tempted when he is drawn away by his own desires and enticed. Then, when desire has conceived, it gives birth to sin; and sin, when it is full-grown, brings forth death... Every good gift and every perfect gift is from above, and comes down from the Father of lights, with whom there is no variation or shadow of turning."* (James 1:13–17)

God is not going to tempt you to sin. Whatever sin you commit is yours. But Scripture also says that God has decreed whatever happens — and sin happens.

Acts 2:23 speaks to this directly: Christ's crucifixion was decreed by God but carried out by sinful men. Peter says:

> *"Him, being delivered by the determined purpose and foreknowledge of God, you have taken by lawless hands, have crucified, and put to death."* (Acts 2:23)

Note the phrase "determined purpose and foreknowledge." There is a Greek grammatical rule called the Granville Sharp rule that indicates the foreknowledge here is defined by the determined purpose. God did not foreknow it because he saw it coming — he foreknew it because he determined it. And yet: "you have taken by lawless hands... and put to death." God appointed it. You are guilty of doing it. Not God.

How do we reconcile this? God is of a different order of existence than we are. He transcends our existence. We cannot decree sin and not be guilty, because we are human. But God, who transcends existence as God, can ordain sin without being guilty of it, because he is not the one committing it. Those who sin are guilty of their sin.

As for free will: the will is free from external compulsion, but the will is controlled by our hearts. Whatever our heart desires, our will pursues. Since we have sinful natures prior to conversion, our will is controlled by that heart — and God's decree includes our sin, because our hearts are full of sin and will perform those sins. It is never a fully satisfying answer, because we always think in human categories. But God transcends this existence. He is the only one who can do what we are describing.

## The Scope of God's Sovereignty

God is sovereign over everything. Let us briefly survey what that includes.

**God is sovereign over nature.** The flood of Genesis 6–9, the plagues of Egypt in Exodus 7–11, Psalm 104 — God controls the weather, plants, animals, and the physical activity of man. Look at Jonah: God is sovereign over the great fish, the storm, the plant, and the worm. In the Gospels, Jesus exercises power over weather, sea, and fish.

**God is sovereign over seemingly trivial or chance events.** Remember the story in 1 Kings 22, where a random arrow hits Ahab and happens to find the gap in his chainmail armor. The man just drew his bowstring by accident. Yet earlier in the chapter, the prophet Micaiah had declared exactly what would happen to Ahab in battle, by the sovereign will of God. Proverbs 16:33 says: "The lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from the Lord." Even the falling of a sparrow — the most common bird there is — does not happen apart from the sovereign will of God.

**God is sovereign over the affairs of individuals and nations.** Proverbs 16:9: "A man's heart plans his way, but the Lord directs his steps." Daniel 2:21: "He changes the times and the seasons; He removes kings and raises up kings."

**God is sovereign over the free actions of people.** Exodus 12:36: "The Lord had given the people favor in the sight of the Egyptians, so that they granted them what they requested." God put it in the Egyptians' hearts to let Israel take the gold. Romans 9:17–18 shows God controlling the heart of Pharaoh.

**God is sovereign over the moral actions of creatures** — both sinful and good. Genesis 45: Joseph tells his brothers, "It was God who sent me here before you." God was sovereign over the brothers' sinful act of selling Joseph to the Ishmaelite caravan. 2 Samuel 24:1: "The anger of the Lord was aroused against Israel, and He moved David against them to say, 'Go, number Israel and Judah'" — which was a sinful action on David's part, and yet God was sovereign over it. 1 Chronicles 21:1 tells us that Satan tempted David to do it. God is also sovereign over Satan's actions — which is encouraging: Satan only does what God appoints him to do.

**God is sovereign over the good actions of men as well.** Ephesians 2:10 says we were redeemed to perform good works that were prepared beforehand for us to walk in.

## Four Practical Applications

**First, great comfort in suffering.** There is no meaningless event. Nothing that happens is meaningless, because it is appointed by a God who is sovereign and good.

**Second, humility in salvation.** We are not saved because there is anything good in us, but because God appointed that we be saved from the foundation of the world. Grace is rooted in God's eternal purpose.

**Third, confidence in prayer.** Prayer is an ordained means by which God brings his decree to pass. God not only appointed what is going to happen — he also appointed how it is going to happen. One of those appointed means is our prayers.

**Fourth, responsibility in obedience.** Sovereignty never excuses sin. We are still accountable for our own sin. Our sin is ours, no one else's. So responsibility in obedience continues as an application of this teaching.
